title: New non-Euclidean neural quantum states from additional types of hyperbolic recurrent neural networks
abstract: In this work, we extend the class of previously introduced non-Euclidean neural quantum states (NQS) which consists only of Poincaré hyperbolic GRU, to new variants including Poincaré RNN as well as Lorentz RNN and Lorentz GRU. In addition to constructing and introducing the new non-Euclidean hyperbolic NQS ansatzes, we generalized the results of our earlier work regarding the definitive outperformances delivered by hyperbolic Poincaré GRU NQS ansatzes when benchmarked against their Euclidean counterparts in the Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) experiments involving the quantum many-body settings of the Heisenberg $J_1J_2$ and $J_1J_2J_3$ models, which exhibit hierarchical structures in the forms of the different degrees of nearest-neighbor interactions. Here, in particular, using larger systems consisting of 100 spins, we found that all four hyperbolic RNN/GRU NQS variants always outperformed their respective Euclidean counterparts. Specifically, for all $J_2$ and $(J_2,J_3)$ couplings considered, including $J_2=0.0$, Lorentz RNN NQS and Poincaré RNN NQS always outperformd Euclidean RNN NQS, while Lorentz/Poincaré GRU NQS always outperformed Euclidean GRU NQS, with a single exception when $J_2=0.0$ for Poincaré GRU NQS. Furthermore, among the four hyperbolic NQS ansatzes, depending on the specific $J_2$ or $(J_2, J_3)$ couplings, on four out of eight experiment settings, Lorentz GRU and Poincaré GRU took turns to be the top performing variant among all Euclidean and hyperbolic NQS ansatzes considered, while Lorentz RNN, with up to three times fewer parameters, was capable of not only surpassing the Euclidean GRU eight out of eight times but also outperforming both Lorentz GRU and Poincaré GRU four out of eight times, to emerge as the best overall hyperbolic NQS ansatz.
summary: This paper introduces new hyperbolic neural network architectures (Poincaré and Lorentz RNN/GRU) for use as Neural Quantum States. By applying these to 1D Heisenberg spin chains, the author demonstrates that hyperbolic geometries significantly outperform standard Euclidean networks in approximating ground state energies. Notably, the Lorentz RNN achieves superior performance with much fewer parameters. The work also addresses critical numerical stability issues inherent in hyperbolic manifolds.
main_problem: The study aims to extend non-Euclidean Neural Quantum States (NQS) by introducing new hyperbolic architectures, specifically Poincaré RNN, Lorentz RNN, Lorentz GRU, and Poincaré GRU, to better approximate quantum many-body ground states.
main_result: All four new hyperbolic RNN/GRU variants consistently outperformed their Euclidean counterparts in 100-spin Heisenberg models, with the Lorentz RNN emerging as the most efficient and high-performing ansatz despite having significantly fewer parameters.
method: The researchers used Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) to train the neural network ansatzes and compared their ground state energy estimates against benchmarks from the Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) method.
model_or_system: The study focuses on 1D Heisenberg J1-J2 and J1-J2-J3 spin models with 100 spins, exploring various coupling regimes that introduce hierarchical structures and frustration.
key_observables: Ground state energy, local energy, and probability distribution.
important_parameters: Coupling constants J2 and J3; spatial constraints Rmax (for Poincaré) and Lmax (for Lorentz) used to prevent numerical instabilities.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the applicability of the Marshall sign for unfrustrated systems and focuses on 1D geometries; the optimal spatial constraints were determined via trial-and-error rather than exhaustive scanning.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the autoregressive calculation process for real and complex NQS, the impact of spatial constraints (Rmax and Lmax) on numerical stability, and training curves comparing energy convergence across different architectures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the mathematical framework for hyperbolic RNN/GRU architectures, details the VMC implementation for real and complex wavefunctions, presents benchmarking results against Euclidean models and DMRG, and discusses numerical stability and future directions.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and simulation as it demonstrates that non-Euclidean geometries can capture complex, hierarchical correlations in quantum systems more efficiently than standard Euclidean neural networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 3: Comparisons of the performances of Euclidean RNN, Lorentz and Poincar´e hyperbolic RNN ansatzes listed in Table 1 for the 1D Heisenberg J1J2 model with J1 = 1.0 and different J2 couplings - clockwise from top: J2 = 0.0, 0.2, 0.8, 0.5. Each dot with error bar represents the mean E value of an NQS ansatz.

Figure 4: Comparisons of the performances of Euclidean GRU, Lorentz and Poincar´e hyperbolic GRU ansatzes listed in Table 1 for the 1D Heisenberg J1J2 model with J1 = 1.0 and different J2 couplings - clockwise from top: J2 = 0.0, 0.2, 0.8, 0.5. Each dot with error bar represents the mean E value of an NQS ansatz.

Figure 5: Comparisons of all six NQS ansatzes, sorted in an ascending performance order for the 1D Heisenberg J1J2 model with J1 = 1.0 and different J2 couplings - top to bottom: J2 = 0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8. Note that all RNN variants have 5394 parameters, while all GRU variants have 15614 parameters.

Figure 6: Comparisons of the performances of Euclidean RNN, Lorentz and Poincar´e hyperbolic RNN ansatzes listed in Table 4 for the 1D Heisenberg J1J2J3 model with J1 = 1.0 and different (J2, J3) couplings - clockwise from top: (0.0, 0.5),(0.2,0.2), (0.5,0.2), (0.2,0.5). All RNN variants have the same 6964 parameters. Each dot with error bar represents the mean E value of an NQS ansatz.

Figure 7: Comparisons of the performances of Euclidean GRU, Lorentz and Poincar´e hyperbolic GRU ansatzes listed in Table 4 for the 1D Heisenberg J1J2J3 model with J1 = 1.0 and different (J2, J3) couplings - clockwise from top: (0.0, 0.5),(0.2,0.2), (0.5,0.2), (0.2,0.5). All GRU variants have the same 15614 parameters. Each dot with error bar represents the mean E value of an NQS ansatz.

Figure 8: Comparisons of all NQS ansatzes, sorted in an ascending performance order for the 1D Heisenberg J1J2J3 model with J1 = 1.0 and different (J2, J3) couplings - top to bottom: (J2, J3) = (0.0, 0.5), (0.2, 0.2), (0.2, 0.5), (0.5, 0.2). Note that the RNN variants have 6964 parameters while the GRU variants have 15614 parameters.

Figure 9: Training curves of RNN-variant NQS ansatzes for the 1D Heisenberg J1J2 model with J1 = 1.0 and different J2 couplings - clockwise J2 = 0.0, 0.2, 0.8, 0.5. The maximum number of epochs is fixed at 1000, but due to the early stopping mechanism that automatically terminated the training if no improvement was registered for the 200 epochs, the actual numbers of epochs run are different for different NQS ansatzes. Euclidean RNN always terminated first upon converging to a local mininum much higher than the true ground state. When present, the inset plot within each plot shows a magnified segment towards the end of the training when the models neared convergence. These inset plots are...

Figure 10: Training curves of GRU-variant NQS ansatzes for the 1D Heisenberg J1J2 model with J1 = 1.0 and different J2 couplings - clockwise J2 = 0.0, 0.2, 0.8, 0.5. The maximum number of epochs is fixed at 1000, but due to the early stopping mechanism that automatically terminated the training if no improvement was registered for the 200 epochs, the actual numbers of epochs run are different for different NQS ansatzes. When present, the inset plot within each plot shows a magnified segment towards the end of the training when the models neared convergence. These inset plots are only included when the curves are not sufficiently distinct in the main plots.
